misconceptionSummary: Students think owing more money (a bigger debt) means having more — they confuse magnitude with value.

visuals.ruleText: A bigger debt means less money — a larger negative digit means a smaller value
visuals.subText: −£7 in your account is worse than −£3

steps.narration: Imagine a bank balance. Negative seven pounds means you owe seven pounds. Negative three pounds means you owe only three. Owing more is worse — so negative seven is the smaller balance.
The distance from zero is called the absolute value. A larger absolute value on the negative side puts the number further left — and further left always means smaller.

lines.text: The inequality sign flips when both sides are negative

steps.narration: The comparison reverses for negatives. Seven is greater than three, but negative seven is less than negative three. The negative sign flips the ordering.

visuals.label: Larger digit after − → further left → smaller value

steps.narration: Negative seven is less than negative three. More debt, further left, smaller value — all three descriptions say the same thing.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: thinking −7 > −3 because 7 > 3. Negatives reverse the order — a larger digit means a smaller, more negative value.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: For negatives, size works in reverse. Think of debt: owing £7 is worse than owing £3. Negative seven is the smaller number.
